settings.titleDesc: Resource pressure via PSI
Overall CPU busy percentage (averaged across all cores)
System load over all CPU cores together
Real RAM usage excluding cache and reclaimable memory
Percentage of swap space currently used by the system
Used Root FS
CPU time spent busy vs idle, split by activity type
RAM and swap usage overview, including caches
Per-interface network traffic (receive and transmit) in bits per second
Percentage of filesystem space used for each mounted device
CPU time usage split by state, normalized across all CPU cores

colors.key: Idle - Waiting for something to happen
colors.name: Idle - Waiting for something to happen

colors.key: Iowait - Waiting for I/O to complete
colors.name: Iowait - Waiting for I/O to complete

colors.key: Nice - Niced processes executing in user mode
colors.name: Nice - Niced processes executing in user mode

colors.key: Steal - Time spent in other operating systems when running in a virtualized environment
colors.name: Steal - Time spent in other operating systems when running in a virtualized environment

colors.key: System - Processes executing in kernel mode
colors.name: System - Processes executing in kernel mode

colors.key: User - Normal processes executing in user mode
colors.name: User - Normal processes executing in user mode

alias.alias: System - Processes executing in kernel mode
alias.name: promql_1

alias.alias: User - Normal processes executing in user mode
alias.name: promql_2

alias.alias: Nice - Niced processes executing in user mode
alias.name: promql_3

alias.alias: Iowait - Waiting for I/O to complete
alias.name: promql_4

alias.alias: Steal - Time spent in other operating systems when running in a virtualized environment
alias.name: promql_7

alias.alias: Idle - Waiting for something to happen
alias.name: promql_8

queries.name: System - Processes executing in kernel mode
User - Normal processes executing in user mode
Nice - Niced processes executing in user mode
Iowait - Waiting for I/O to complete
Steal - Time spent in other operating systems when running in a virtualized environment
Idle - Waiting for something to happen

query.q: sum by(instance) (rate(node_cpu_guest_seconds_total{instance="#{node}"}[$__rate_interval])) / on(instance) group_left sum by (instance)((rate(node_cpu_seconds_total{instance="#{node}"}[$__rate_interval]))) > 0

settings.titleDesc: Breakdown of physical memory and swap usage. Hardware-detected memory errors are also displayed

colors.key: Hardware Corrupted - Amount of RAM that the kernel identified as corrupted / not working
colors.name: Hardware Corrupted - Amount of RAM that the kernel identified as corrupted / not working

alias.alias: Apps - Memory used by user-space applications
alias.name: promql_1

alias.alias: PageTables - Memory used to map between virtual and physical memory addresses
alias.name: promql_2

alias.alias: SwapCache - Memory that keeps track of pages that have been fetched from swap but not yet been modified
alias.name: promql_3

alias.alias: Slab - Memory used by the kernel to cache data structures for its own use (caches like inode, dentry, etc)
alias.name: promql_4

alias.alias: Cache - Parked file data (file content) cache
alias.name: promql_5

alias.alias: Hardware Corrupted - Amount of RAM that the kernel identified as corrupted / not working
alias.name: promql_9

queries.name: Apps - Memory used by user-space applications
PageTables - Memory used to map between virtual and physical memory addresses
SwapCache - Memory that keeps track of pages that have been fetched from swap but not yet been modified
Slab - Memory used by the kernel to cache data structures for its own use (caches like inode, dentry, etc)
Cache - Parked file data (file content) cache
Hardware Corrupted - Amount of RAM that the kernel identified as corrupted / not working

settings.titleDesc: Incoming and outgoing network traffic per interface
Network interface utilization as a percentage of its maximum capacity
Disk I/O operations per second for each device
Disk I/O throughput per device
Amount of available disk space per mounted filesystem, excluding rootfs. Based on block availability to non-root users
Disk usage (used = total - available) per mountpoint
Percentage of time the disk was actively processing I/O operations

How often tasks experience CPU, memory, or I/O delays. 'Some' indicates partial slowdown; 'Full' indicates all tasks are stalled. Based on Linux PSI metrics:
https://docs.kernel.org/accounting/psi.html
Displays committed memory usage versus the system's commit limit. Exceeding the limit is allowed under Linux overcommit policies but may increase OOM risks under high load

alias.alias: Committed_AS – Memory promised to processes (not necessarily used)
alias.name: promql_1

queries.name: Committed_AS – Memory promised to processes (not necessarily used)

settings.titleDesc: Memory currently dirty (modified but not yet written to disk), being actively written back, or held by writeback buffers. High dirty or writeback memory may indicate disk I/O pressure or delayed flushing

alias.alias: Writeback – Memory currently being flushed to disk
alias.name: promql_1

alias.alias: Dirty – Memory marked dirty (pending write to disk)
alias.name: promql_3

alias.alias: NFS Unstable – Pages sent to NFS server, awaiting storage commit
alias.name: promql_4

queries.name: Writeback – Memory currently being flushed to disk
Dirty – Memory marked dirty (pending write to disk)
NFS Unstable – Pages sent to NFS server, awaiting storage commit

settings.titleDesc: Kernel slab memory usage, separated into reclaimable and non-reclaimable categories. Reclaimable memory can be freed under memory pressure (e.g., caches), while unreclaimable memory is locked by the kernel for core functions

alias.alias: SReclaimable – Potentially reclaimable slab memory (e.g., inode cache)
alias.name: promql_2

queries.name: SReclaimable – Potentially reclaimable slab memory (e.g., inode cache)

settings.titleDesc: Memory used for mapped files (such as libraries) and shared memory (shmem and tmpfs), including variants backed by huge pages

alias.alias: Mapped – Memory mapped from files (e.g., libraries, mmap)
alias.name: promql_1

alias.alias: Shmem – Shared memory used by processes and tmpfs
alias.name: promql_2

alias.alias: ShmemHugePages – Shared memory (shmem/tmpfs) allocated with HugePages
alias.name: promql_3

alias.alias: PMD Mapped – Shmem/tmpfs backed by Transparent HugePages (PMD)
alias.name: promql_4

queries.name: Mapped – Memory mapped from files (e.g., libraries, mmap)
Shmem – Shared memory used by processes and tmpfs
ShmemHugePages – Shared memory (shmem/tmpfs) allocated with HugePages
PMD Mapped – Shmem/tmpfs backed by Transparent HugePages (PMD)

settings.titleDesc: Proportion of memory pages in the kernel's active and inactive LRU lists relative to total RAM. Active pages have been recently used, while inactive pages are less recently accessed but still resident in memory

alias.alias: Inactive – Less recently used memory, more likely to be reclaimed
alias.name: promql_1

alias.alias: Active – Recently used memory, retained unless under pressure
alias.name: promql_2

queries.name: Inactive – Less recently used memory, more likely to be reclaimed
Active – Recently used memory, retained unless under pressure

settings.titleDesc: Breakdown of memory pages in the kernel's active and inactive LRU lists, separated by anonymous (heap, tmpfs) and file-backed (caches, mmap) pages.

alias.alias: Inactive_file - File-backed memory on inactive LRU list
alias.name: promql_1

alias.alias: Inactive_anon – Anonymous memory on inactive LRU (incl. tmpfs & swap cache)
alias.name: promql_2

alias.alias: Active_file - File-backed memory on active LRU list
alias.name: promql_3

alias.alias: Active_anon – Anonymous memory on active LRU (incl. tmpfs & swap cache)
alias.name: promql_4

queries.name: Inactive_file - File-backed memory on inactive LRU list
Inactive_anon – Anonymous memory on inactive LRU (incl. tmpfs & swap cache)
Active_file - File-backed memory on active LRU list
Active_anon – Anonymous memory on active LRU (incl. tmpfs & swap cache)

settings.titleDesc: Tracks kernel memory used for CPU-local structures, per-thread stacks, and bounce buffers used for I/O on DMA-limited devices. These areas are typically small but critical for low-level operations

alias.alias: PerCPU – Dynamically allocated per-CPU memory (used by kernel modules)
alias.name: promql_2

alias.alias: Bounce Memory – I/O buffer for DMA-limited devices
alias.name: promql_3

queries.name: PerCPU – Dynamically allocated per-CPU memory (used by kernel modules)
Bounce Memory – I/O buffer for DMA-limited devices

settings.titleDesc: Usage of the kernel's vmalloc area, which provides virtual memory allocations for kernel modules and drivers. Includes total, used, and largest free block sizes

alias.alias: Vmalloc Free Chunk – Largest available block in vmalloc area
alias.name: promql_1

alias.alias: Vmalloc Total – Total size of the vmalloc memory area
alias.name: promql_2

alias.alias: Vmalloc Used – Portion of vmalloc area currently in use
alias.name: promql_3

queries.name: Vmalloc Free Chunk – Largest available block in vmalloc area
Vmalloc Total – Total size of the vmalloc memory area
Vmalloc Used – Portion of vmalloc area currently in use

settings.titleDesc: Memory used by anonymous pages (not backed by files), including standard and huge page allocations. Includes heap, stack, and memory-mapped anonymous regions
Memory that is locked in RAM and cannot be swapped out. Includes both kernel-unevictable memory and user-level memory locked with mlock()
How much memory is directly mapped in the kernel using different page sizes (4K, 2M, 1G). Helps monitor large page utilization in the direct map region
Displays HugePages memory usage in bytes, including allocated, free, reserved, and surplus memory. All values are calculated based on the number of huge pages multiplied by their configured size
Rate of memory pages being read from or written to disk (page-in and page-out operations). High page-out may indicate memory pressure or swapping activity
Rate at which memory pages are being swapped in from or out to disk. High swap-out activity may indicate memory pressure
Rate of memory page faults, split into total, major (disk-backed), and derived minor (non-disk) faults. High major fault rates may indicate memory pressure or insufficient RAM

colors.key: Pgfault - Page major and minor fault ops
colors.name: Pgfault - Page major and minor fault ops

alias.alias: Pgfault - Page major and minor fault ops
alias.name: promql_1

queries.name: Pgfault - Page major and minor fault ops

settings.titleDesc: Rate of Out-of-Memory (OOM) kill events. A non-zero value indicates the kernel has terminated one or more processes due to memory exhaustion
Tracks the system clock's estimated and maximum error, as well as its offset from the reference clock (e.g., via NTP). Useful for detecting synchronization drift
NTP phase-locked loop (PLL) time constant used by the kernel to control time adjustments. Lower values mean faster correction but less stability
Shows whether the system clock is synchronized to a reliable time source, and the current frequency correction ratio applied by the kernel to maintain synchronization
Displays the PPS signal's frequency offset and stability (jitter) in hertz. Useful for monitoring high-precision time sources like GPS or atomic clocks
Tracks PPS signal timing jitter and shift compared to system clock
Rate of PPS synchronization diagnostics including calibration events, jitter violations, errors, and frequency stability exceedances
Processes currently in runnable or blocked states. Helps identify CPU contention or I/O wait bottlenecks.
Current number of processes in each state (e.g., running, sleeping, zombie). Requires --collector.processes to be enabled in node_exporter
Rate of new processes being created on the system (forks/sec).
Shows CPU saturation per core, calculated as the proportion of time spent waiting to run relative to total time demanded (running + waiting).
Number of active PIDs on the system and the configured maximum allowed. Useful for detecting PID exhaustion risk. Requires --collector.processes in node_exporter
Number of active threads on the system and the configured thread limit. Useful for monitoring thread pressure. Requires --collector.processes in node_exporter
Per-second rate of context switches and hardware interrupts. High values may indicate intense CPU or I/O activity
System load average over 1, 5, and 15 minutes. Reflects the number of active or waiting processes. Values above CPU core count may indicate overload
Real-time CPU frequency scaling per core, including average minimum and maximum allowed scaling frequencies
Rate of scheduling timeslices executed per CPU. Reflects how frequently the scheduler switches tasks on each core
Breaks down hardware interrupts by type and device. Useful for diagnosing IRQ load on network, disk, or CPU interfaces. Requires --collector.interrupts to be enabled in node_exporter

Number of bits of entropy currently available to the system's random number generators (e.g., /dev/random). Low values may indicate that random number generation could block or degrade performance of cryptographic operations
Monitors hardware sensor temperatures and critical thresholds as exposed by Linux hwmon. Includes CPU, GPU, and motherboard sensors where available
Shows how hard each cooling device (fan/throttle) is working relative to its maximum capacity
Shows the online status of power supplies (e.g., AC, battery). A value of 1-Yes indicates the power supply is active/online
Displays the current fan speeds (RPM) from hardware sensors via the hwmon interface
Current number of systemd units in each operational state, such as active, failed, inactive, or transitioning
Current number of active connections per systemd socket, as reported by the Node Exporter systemd collector
Rate of accepted connections per second for each systemd socket
Rate of systemd socket connection refusals per second, typically due to service unavailability or backlog overflow
Number of I/O operations completed per second for the device (after merges), including both reads and writes
Number of bytes read from or written to the device per second
Average time for requests issued to the device to be served. This includes the time spent by the requests in queue and the time spent servicing them.
Average queue length of the requests that were issued to the device
Number of read and write requests merged per second that were queued to the device
Percentage of time the disk spent actively processing I/O operations, including general I/O, discards (TRIM), and write cache flushes
Per-second rate of discard (TRIM) and flush (write cache) operations. Useful for monitoring low-level disk activity on SSDs and advanced storage
Shows how many disk sectors are discarded (TRIMed) per second. Useful for monitoring SSD behavior and storage efficiency
Number of in-progress I/O requests at the time of sampling (active requests in the disk queue)
Number of file descriptors currently allocated system-wide versus the system limit. Important for detecting descriptor exhaustion risks
Number of free file nodes (inodes) available per mounted filesystem. A low count may prevent file creation even if disk space is available
Indicates filesystems mounted in read-only mode or reporting device-level I/O errors.
Number of file nodes (inodes) available per mounted filesystem. Reflects maximum file capacity regardless of disk size
Number of network packets received and transmitted per second, by interface.
Rate of packet-level errors for each network interface. Receive errors may indicate physical or driver issues; transmit errors may reflect collisions or hardware faults
Rate of dropped packets per network interface. Receive drops can indicate buffer overflow or driver issues; transmit drops may result from outbound congestion or queuing limits
Rate of compressed network packets received and transmitted per interface. These are common in low-bandwidth or special interfaces like PPP or SLIP
Rate of incoming multicast packets received per network interface. Multicast is used by protocols such as mDNS, SSDP, and some streaming or cluster services
Rate of received packets that could not be processed due to missing protocol or handler in the kernel. May indicate unsupported traffic or misconfiguration
Rate of frame errors on received packets, typically caused by physical layer issues such as bad cables, duplex mismatches, or hardware problems
Tracks FIFO buffer overrun errors on network interfaces. These occur when incoming or outgoing packets are dropped due to queue or buffer overflows, often indicating congestion or hardware limits
Rate of packet collisions detected during transmission. Mostly relevant on half-duplex or legacy Ethernet networks
Rate of carrier errors during transmission. These typically indicate physical layer issues like faulty cabling or duplex mismatches
Number of ARP entries per interface. Useful for detecting excessive ARP traffic or table growth due to scanning or misconfiguration
Current and maximum connection tracking entries used by Netfilter (nf_conntrack). High usage approaching the limit may cause packet drops or connection issues
Operational and physical link status of each network interface. Values are Yes for 'up' or link present, and No for 'down' or no carrier.
Maximum speed of each network interface as reported by the operating system. This is a static hardware capability, not current throughput
MTU (Maximum Transmission Unit) in bytes for each network interface. Affects packet size and transmission efficiency
Tracks TCP socket usage and memory per node
Number of UDP and UDPLite sockets currently in use
Total number of sockets currently in use across all protocols (TCP, UDP, UNIX, etc.), as reported by /proc/net/sockstat
Number of FRAG and RAW sockets currently in use. RAW sockets are used for custom protocols or tools like ping; FRAG sockets are used internally for IP packet defragmentation
Kernel memory used by TCP, UDP, and IP fragmentation buffers
Average memory used per socket (TCP/UDP). Helps tune net.ipv4.tcp_rmem / tcp_wmem
TCP/UDP socket memory usage in kernel (in pages)
Packets processed and dropped by the softnet network stack per CPU. Drops may indicate CPU saturation or network driver limitations
How often the kernel was unable to process all packets in the softnet queue before time ran out. Frequent squeezes may indicate CPU contention or driver inefficiency
Tracks the number of packets processed or dropped by Receive Packet Steering (RPS), a mechanism to distribute packet processing across CPUs
Rate of octets sent and received at the IP layer, as reported by /proc/net/netstat
Rate of TCP segments sent and received per second, including data and control segments
Rate of UDP datagrams sent and received per second, based on /proc/net/netstat
Number of ICMP messages sent and received per second, including error and control messages
Tracks various TCP error and congestion-related events, including retransmissions, timeouts, dropped connections, and buffer issues
Rate of UDP and UDPLite datagram delivery errors, including missing listeners, buffer overflows, and protocol-specific issues
Rate of incoming ICMP messages that contained protocol-specific errors, such as bad checksums or invalid lengths
Rate of TCP SYN cookies sent, validated, and failed. These are used to protect against SYN flood attacks and manage TCP handshake resources under load
Number of currently established TCP connections and the system's max supported limit. On Linux, MaxConn may return -1 to indicate a dynamic/unlimited configuration
Number of UDP packets currently queued in the receive (RX) and transmit (TX) buffers. A growing queue may indicate a bottleneck
Rate of TCP connection initiations per second. 'Active' opens are initiated by this host. 'Passive' opens are accepted from incoming connections
Number of TCP sockets in key connection states. Requires the --collector.tcpstat flag on node_exporter
Transient TCP connection states. These are typically short-lived during connection establishment and teardown
TCP socket queue sizes. High rx_queued_bytes indicates application not reading fast enough. High tx_queued_bytes indicates network congestion or slow receiver
Duration of each individual collector executed during a Node Exporter scrape. Useful for identifying slow or failing collectors
Rate of CPU time used by the process exposing this metric (user + system mode)
Tracks the memory usage of the process exposing this metric (e.g., node_exporter), including current virtual memory and maximum virtual memory limit
Number of file descriptors used by the exporter process versus its configured limit
Shows whether each Node Exporter collector scraped successfully (1 = success, 0 = failure), and whether the textfile collector returned an error.
